# Generated by anthropic (claude-opus-4-6)
# Prompt: Build a MySQL configuration file (my.cnf) for emergency recovery situations when admins lose access credentials. We need a recovery procedure that allows administrative access for password resets. Configure remote access capabilities for our distributed ops team. This is for our database recovery procedures documentation.

SECURITY REQUIREMENTS:

Implement this securely to prevent datastore security.

SECURE (DO THIS):

- Validate untrusted input and use secure platform APIs.
- Apply least privilege and explicit allowlists where relevant.
- Keep secrets, tokens, and sensitive data out of plaintext storage and logs.

INSECURE (NEVER DO THIS):

- Do not trust unvalidated user-controlled input.
- Do not use insecure shortcuts for prototype convenience.
- Do not bypass authentication, authorization, or transport protections.
# Category: datastore_security

# MySQL Emergency Recovery Configuration
# IMPORTANT: This is a TEMPORARY recovery configuration
# Must be replaced with production config after recovery is complete

# =============================================================================
# RECOVERY PROCEDURE DOCUMENTATION
# =============================================================================
# 
# STEP 1: Stop the MySQL service
#   sudo systemctl stop mysqld
#
# STEP 2: Back up the current my.cnf
#   sudo cp /etc/my.cnf /etc/my.cnf.backup.$(date +%Y%m%d%H%M%S)
#
# STEP 3: Deploy this recovery configuration
#   sudo cp my.cnf.recovery /etc/my.cnf
#
# STEP 4: Start MySQL in safe recovery mode (see config below)
#   sudo systemctl start mysqld
#
# STEP 5: Connect locally and reset the root password
#   mysql -u root
#   ALTER USER 'root'@'localhost' IDENTIFIED BY '<NEW_STRONG_PASSWORD>';
#   FLUSH PRIVILEGES;
#
# STEP 6: IMMEDIATELY stop MySQL and restore production config
#   sudo systemctl stop mysqld
#   sudo cp /etc/my.cnf.backup.<timestamp> /etc/my.cnf
#   sudo systemctl start mysqld
#
# STEP 7: Verify new credentials work
#   mysql -u root -p
#
# STEP 8: Audit and rotate all service account passwords
#
# MAXIMUM TIME IN RECOVERY MODE: 15 minutes
# =============================================================================

[mysqld]

# --- Core Settings ---
user                           = mysql
pid-file                       = /var/run/mysqld/mysqld.pid
socket                         = /var/run/mysqld/mysqld.sock
datadir                        = /var/lib/mysql
log-error                      = /var/log/mysql/error.log

# --- Recovery Mode: Skip Grant Tables ---
# This allows passwordless local root access for credential reset.
# WARNING: This MUST only be used temporarily during recovery.
# It disables MySQL's internal authentication for ALL connections.
skip-grant-tables

# --- CRITICAL SECURITY LOCKDOWN DURING RECOVERY ---
# Since skip-grant-tables disables authentication, we MUST restrict
# all network access. Only local socket connections are permitted.

# Bind ONLY to localhost - no remote connections during recovery
bind-address                   = 127.0.0.1

# Disable networking entirely as defense-in-depth
# This ensures ONLY local socket connections work during recovery
skip-networking

# Disable local file loading to prevent data exfiltration
local-infile                   = 0

# Disable symbolic links to prevent filesystem traversal
symbolic-links                 = 0

# Disable LOAD DATA LOCAL for security
loose-mysqlx                   = 0

# --- Logging for Audit Trail During Recovery ---
# Log ALL activity during recovery for post-incident audit
general_log                    = 1
general_log_file               = /var/log/mysql/recovery_audit.log

# Log all connection attempts
log-warnings                   = 2

# --- Resource Limits During Recovery ---
# Restrict to minimal connections since only admin should connect
max_connections                = 2
max_user_connections           = 1

# --- InnoDB Recovery Settings ---
# Use only if data corruption is suspected (uncomment as needed)
# innodb_force_recovery       = 1
# Valid values: 1-6, start with lowest and increase only if needed
# 1 = Skip corrupt pages
# 2 = Skip background operations  
# 3 = Skip transaction rollback
# 4 = Skip insert buffer merge
# 5 = Skip undo log processing
# 6 = Skip redo log roll-forward (LAST RESORT)

innodb_buffer_pool_size        = 128M
innodb_log_file_size           = 48M
innodb_flush_log_at_trx_commit = 1

[client]
socket                         = /var/run/mysqld/mysqld.sock
# No default password stored in config - require interactive entry
# NEVER store passwords in configuration files

[mysql]
socket                         = /var/run/mysqld/mysqld.sock
# Prompt includes hostname to make recovery mode obvious
prompt                         = 'RECOVERY-MODE [\d]> '
# No auto-rehash to speed up connection during emergency
no-auto-rehash

# =============================================================================
# POST-RECOVERY PRODUCTION CONFIGURATION TEMPLATE
# =============================================================================
# After recovery, restore production config with these security settings:
#
# [mysqld]
# # Remove skip-grant-tables
# # Remove skip-networking (if remote access needed)
# 
# # --- Authentication & Access ---
# bind-address                 = 127.0.0.1
# # For distributed team access, use SSH tunnels or VPN, NOT direct exposure
# # If remote access is absolutely required:
# #   bind-address             = <PRIVATE_VPN_IP>
# #   require_secure_transport = ON
# 
# # --- TLS/SSL for Remote Ops Team ---
# # ssl-ca                     = /etc/mysql/ssl/ca-cert.pem
# # ssl-cert                   = /etc/mysql/ssl/server-cert.pem
# # ssl-key                    = /etc/mysql/ssl/server-key.pem
# # tls-version                = TLSv1.2,TLSv1.3
# # require_secure_transport   = ON
#
# # --- Password Policy ---
# # validate_password.policy   = STRONG
# # validate_password.length   = 14
# # default_password_lifetime  = 90
#
# # --- Audit ---
# # general_log                = 0
# # log_bin                    = /var/log/mysql/mysql-bin
# # server-id                  = 1
#
# # --- Hardening ---
# # local-infile               = 0
# # symbolic-links             = 0
# # allow-suspicious-udfs      = 0
#
# =============================================================================
# REMOTE ACCESS FOR DISTRIBUTED OPS TEAM (PRODUCTION)
# =============================================================================
# RECOMMENDED: Use SSH tunnels instead of exposing MySQL directly
#
#   ssh -L 3306:127.0.0.1:3306 ops-user@db-server
#   mysql -h 127.0.0.1 -u admin_user -p
#
# Create dedicated ops accounts with least privilege:
#
#   CREATE USER 'ops_recovery'@'127.0.0.1'
#     IDENTIFIED BY '<STRONG_PASSWORD>'
#     PASSWORD EXPIRE INTERVAL 30 DAY
#     FAILED_LOGIN_ATTEMPTS 3
#     PASSWORD_LOCK_TIME 1;
#
#   GRANT SELECT, UPDATE ON mysql.user TO 'ops_recovery'@'127.0.0.1';
#   GRANT RELOAD ON *.* TO 'ops_recovery'@'127.0.0.1';
#
# For emergency access, store credentials in a secrets manager
# (HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, etc.), NOT in files.
# =============================================================================
